title: Quasinormal mode quantization of bound and propagating photons in complex lightguiding nanostructures for integrated devices
abstract: Open optical or plasmonic resonators are placed on and connected through surfaces or via waveguides, forming complex lightguiding nanostructures, e.g. for integrated photonic quantum devices. We derive general boundary conditions for quasinormal modes that account for the structure's specific geometry. We then present a general quantization scheme for multiple, interacting quasinormal-mode cavities coupled to quantum emitters and to a non-bosonic bath of propagating photons on waveguides or a surface. We derive a system-bath Hamiltonian with rigorously defined coupling elements that can be computed using Maxwell solvers, including light-matter coupling between the electromagnetic field and quantum emitters. We define system-bath correlation functions for an effective, bath-mediated, and time-delayed interaction between the quasinormal modes and quantum emitters, which is a main ingredient commonly used to simulate open quantum system dynamics.
summary: This paper develops a rigorous theory for quantizing light in complex, lossy nanostructures like waveguides and surfaces. It provides a way to calculate how cavities and quantum emitters interact with each other and with propagating photons. This is crucial for the accurate simulation of integrated quantum optical devices.
main_problem: The lack of a rigorous quantization framework for quasinormal modes (QNMs) in complex, lossy, and structured lightguiding environments like waveguides and surfaces.
main_result: A generalized quantization scheme and derivation of boundary conditions for QNMs that accounts for structured backgrounds, including a system-bath Hamiltonian with rigorously defined coupling elements.
method: The authors use the electromagnetic Green's function, Green's second identity, and a regularization scheme to transform diverging QNM fields into regularized, computable forms.
model_or_system: Multiple interacting QNM cavities (e.g., metal dimers or photonic crystal cavities) coupled to quantum emitters and a non-bosonic bath of propagating photons in waveguides, surfaces, or 3D media.
key_observables: Complex eigenfrequencies, coupling elements, system-bath correlation functions, and QNM overlap matrices (intracavity and intercavity).
important_parameters: Complex eigenfrequencies (decay rates), separation parameter (distance between cavities), transfer factor (power transfer between cavities), and group velocity.
main_assumptions: The medium is assumed to be absorptive, isotropic, inhomogeneous, and non-magnetic; the far-field divergence is addressed via a few-mode regularization approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows examples of coupled cavities in different environments (dimers, waveguides, surfaces); Figure 4 illustrates a cavity near a planar interface; Figure 5 demonstrates the decoupling of multiple waveguides.
paper_structure: The paper begins by deriving general boundary conditions for QNMs, extends these to specific geometries (1D, 2D, 3D, and waveguides), presents a quantization scheme for the system-bath Hamiltonian, and concludes with the calculation of overlap integrals and intercavity coupling.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for simulating open quantum system dynamics in realistic, lossy nanophotonic architectures, which is essential for designing integrated quantum photonic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Examples of different types of coupled QNM cavities separated by distance R in various types of background structures. (a) Metal dimers described by a Drude permittivity ϵM in a 3D homogeneous background medium ϵB [19, 29]. (b) Cavities V1, V2 on a photonic crystal with lattice constant a, side-coupled to the same periodic photonic crystal waveguide [38–40]. (c) Plasmonic nanoparticles V1, V2 described by the Drude permittivity ϵM near a planar surface [41, 42].

Figure 2. (a) Closed cavity system. A scattering medium described by permittivity ϵ(r, ω) is placed inside a closed resonator of length L, so that waves will scatter back from the resonator surface, described by the boundary conditions in Eq. (9). (b)-(d) Cavity in a homogeneous background medium for 1D, 2D, and 3D, respectively. In 1D, the condition from Eq. (10) yields outgoing boundary conditions at the cavity surface [Eq. (12)]. For 2D (3D), we choose a circular (spherical) far-field surface for the integral from Eq. (13) [Eq. (8)], which yields the outgoing wave boundary conditions from Eq. (16) [Eq. (20)].

Figure 3. (a) Sketch of a single cylindrical waveguide with forward (backward) propagating waveguide modes f (∗) kj (r) and infinite cross-section A∞over which the normalization of the waveguide modes is carried out [Eq. (22)]. (b) Sketch of a cavity coupled to the waveguide from (a) as its only loss channel. The field of the open cavity extends into the waveguide. Equation (8) is evaluated over a far-field surface Sside ∪S−∪S+, yielding the waveguide boundary conditions from Eq. (24).

Figure 4. Sketch of a cavity (here, a plasmonic particle) located near the planar interface between two different media ϵ1 ̸= ϵ2. (a) As our far-field surface for the boundary condition integral from Eq. (8), we chose a half sphere with radius R →∞. The far field takes different forms, depending on the direction in which the emission occurs. Far away from the surface, the waves behave like spherical waves the form eiκsppr/√r (red arrow) appear, similar to the 2D problem from Sec. 2.4. Here, κspp is the in-plane wavenumber of a surface plasmon polarition [cf. Eq. (30)]. (b) Same as before but with a cylindrical far-field surface with height h →∞and radius ρ →∞. Figure 5. Example for a system with separate background structures. A cavity is coupled to three semi-finite waveguides with different refractive indices n1/2/3. The waveguides can be made from different materials or have different cross-section profiles, thereby supporting very different waveguide modes (indicated by the different colors). If the waveguide modes do not extend into the outside medium [∆G ≈0, cf. Eq. (40)], the three waveguides can be treated separately and the cavity emission inside one of the waveguides obeys the boundary conditions for that waveguide alone [Eq. (24)].
